title: ER to Relational Schema Conversion Algorithm
description: Algorithm to convert ER diagram entities to relational database tables
code:
// ER TO RELATIONAL MODEL CONVERSION
// BCS403 - Database Management System

ALGORITHM Convert_ER_to_Relational
BEGIN
 // Step 1: Convert Strong Entities
 FOR EACH Strong_Entity E DO
 Create relation R with all simple attributes of E
 Include all key attributes as primary key
 Include composite attributes' simple components
 END FOR

 // Step 2: Convert Weak Entities
 FOR EACH Weak_Entity W DO
 Create relation R with all attributes of W
 Include primary key of owner entity as foreign key
 Composite primary key = (owner_key, partial_key)
 END FOR

 // Step 3: Convert Binary 1:1 Relationships
 FOR EACH 1:1 Relationship R DO
 Option 1: Merge with one entity
 Option 2: Create separate relation with foreign keys
 END FOR

 // Step 4: Convert Binary 1:M Relationships
 FOR EACH 1:M Relationship R DO
 Include primary key of 'one' side as foreign key
 Add relationship attributes to 'many' side relation
 END FOR

 // Step 5: Convert Binary M:N Relationships
 FOR EACH M:N Relationship R DO
 Create new relation R_assoc
 Include primary keys of both entities (composite FK)
 Include relationship attributes
 Composite primary key = (PK1, PK2)
 END FOR

 // Step 6: Convert Multi-valued Attributes
 FOR EACH Multi-valued Attribute A DO
 Create new relation R
 Include A as single-valued attribute
 Include primary key of entity as foreign key
 Primary Key = (Entity_PK, A)
 END FOR

END
explanation: This algorithm outlines the steps to convert an ER diagram into relational database schema. Strong entities become tables with primary keys. Weak entities include the owner's primary key. 1:M relationships add foreign key to 'many' side. M:N relationships require an associative table. Multi-valued attributes become separate tables.

complexity.time: O(n) where n is number of entities/relationships

title: Sample SQL for ER Diagram Implementation
description: SQL DDL statements to create tables based on an ER diagram
code:
-- ER DIAGRAM IMPLEMENTATION EXAMPLE
-- Entity: STUDENT (Strong)
-- Entity: COURSE
-- Relationship: ENROLLS (M:N)

-- Creating Student Table
CREATE TABLE Student (
 Student_ID VARCHAR(10) PRIMARY KEY,
 Name VARCHAR(50) NOT NULL,
 Age NUMBER(3),
 Gender CHAR(1),
 Address VARCHAR(100),
 Email VARCHAR(50) UNIQUE
);

-- Creating Course Table
CREATE TABLE Course (
 Course_ID VARCHAR(10) PRIMARY KEY,
 Course_Name VARCHAR(50) NOT NULL,
 Credits NUMBER(2),
 Department VARCHAR(30)
);

-- Creating Enrolment (Associative Entity for M:N)
CREATE TABLE Enrolment (
 Student_ID VARCHAR(10),
 Course_ID VARCHAR(10),
 Enrollment_Date DATE,
 Grade CHAR(2),
 PRIMARY KEY (Student_ID, Course_ID),
 FOREIGN KEY (Student_ID) REFERENCES Student(Student_ID),
 FOREIGN KEY (Course_ID) REFERENCES Course(Course_ID)
);

-- Creating Phone (Multi-valued attribute of Student)
CREATE TABLE Student_Phone (
 Student_ID VARCHAR(10),
 Phone_No VARCHAR(15),
 PRIMARY KEY (Student_ID, Phone_No),
 FOREIGN KEY (Student_ID) REFERENCES Student(Student_ID)
);
explanation: This SQL demonstrates how ER diagram components translate to actual database tables. Student and Course are strong entities. Enrolment is an associative entity resolving the M:N relationship. Student_Phone handles the multi-valued attribute Phone_No.
